content.assessment: From a demographic lens, the status quo preserves a small, geographically concentrated selective sector (2-3% of students, 85% selection-capable schools, urban-skewed distribution) precisely as birth cohorts contract sharply and 270 schools face enrollment-driven consolidation. Without intervention, the counterfactual is not stasis but intensification: shrinking cohorts will concentrate further in urban, elite tracks while ordinary and rural schools absorb disproportionate enrollment decline, deepening the already-extreme segregation pattern (71% between-school variance). Monitoring generates data but has no mechanism to alter the spatial or cohort trajectory.
opportunities: Existing data infrastructure could in principle be repurposed for a future cohort-sensitive redistribution if political will emerges
strengths: Avoids destabilizing an already fragile enrollment landscape amid the closure/consolidation wave
Preserves planning continuity during a period of rapid demographic contraction where capacity for major structural change may be limited
threats: Continued cohort decline (20% birth-count fall in four years) will mechanically shrink the ordinary-school population faster than the insulated elite-track population, worsening relative segregation even absent any policy change
270 endangered small schools face consolidation pressure without any counterfactual policy addressing how declining cohorts are distributed between selective and non-selective tracks
weaknesses: Provides no lever to counteract the geographic concentration of selective tracks (48% small towns, 25% county seats, 23% Budapest, only 3% villages) that insulates elite tracks from rural depopulation
Historical precedent shows PISA/ABC data monitoring has not previously translated into policy correction, so the same infrastructure is unlikely to change trajectory now
